title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
National Pension Scheme for NRI
The National Pension Scheme for NRI was introduced for government employees in 2004 to provide them with financial security after retirement. Starting in 2009, this retirement planning scheme was available for all Indian citizens, including employees in the private sector, self-employed individuals, and non-resident Indians (NRIs). The plan systematically urges people to start saving from an early age and plan their retirement accordingly. It often focuses on accumulating regular contributions to your retirement corpus through low-cost investments.
In this guide, we will discuss more about the features of NPS for NRIs, investment opportunities, and benefits in detail.
What is the National Pension Scheme for NRI?
The National Pension Scheme for NRI is a retirement plan introduced by the Government of India as a part of pension sector reforms. The scheme is a long-term, voluntary retirement savings plan in India which is administered and regulated by the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA). Any NRI who holds a non-resident external (NRE) or non-resident ordinary (NRO) account can invest in NPS to generate a retirement corpus, which is a combination of equity, government securities, and debt. This ensures minimal impact on returns for subscriber contributions, even if the market is down.
In short, an NPS for NRI scheme allows non-resident Indians to accumulate a safe retirement fund in India and incur tax advantages accordingly. Another advantage is that it offers multiple possibilities for NRIs because of its structured investment options.
When the person reaches 60 years, they can easily withdraw up to 60% of the maturity benefit in a single lump sum payment. They can also use the remaining 40% to purchase another annuity scheme for a regular pension.
Key Features of the NPS for NRI Account
The primary objective of the National Pension Scheme for NRI is to promote savings and investment and help individuals plan their retirement. Meanwhile, the following features make it an excellent choice for long-term wealth accumulation:
Flexibility and Portability
The Central Recordkeeping Agency (CRA) issues a Permanent Retirement Account Number (PRAN) to each subscriber of the NPS scheme. This helps them maintain their respective databases along with all recorded transactions until their retirement. PRAN ensures portability and continuity of the pension account across employment, geographies, and sector changes.
Another positive part about the NPS scheme for NRI is that the PRAN remains the same for the subscriber’s lifetime and ensures efficient portability if someone is moving to a new location in India.
Voluntary Contributions
The National Pension Scheme for NRI allows subscribers to participate in investments voluntarily according to their financial capacity. Unlike traditional pension schemes, this retirement plan allows investors to contribute as and when convenient. The minimum investment expected from the NRIs is ₹500 for each transaction and ₹1000 annually, while opening a Tier I account.
There is no cap on the invested amount, which makes NPS an excellent retirement planning scheme for individuals with higher net worth.
Investment Portfolio Options
NRIs can easily customise their portfolios, thanks to the flexibility offered by NPS. This allows better diversification and risk management. The scheme provides multiple investment portfolio options for the subscribers, such as Equity (E), Corporate Bonds (C) and /or Government Securities (G).
The equity option allows investments in stock-market-linked instruments for better long-term returns. Conversely, NRIs can also invest in high-rated corporate bonds issued by several reputed Indian corporates. The Government securities option, on the other hand, focuses on investments across central bonds and treasury bills, which are often considered less risky instruments.
Partial Withdrawals and Exits
NPS for NRI also allows partial withdrawal from the account if needed. Under the Tier I scheme, you will be allowed to withdraw a maximum of 25% of your contributions. The same rule applies if your employer also contributes to your account. There are no such restrictions if you have a Tier II account. However, the amount withdrawn within 3 years is taxable in the year of withdrawal for employees who have claimed deductions under Section 80C.
Eligibility Criteria for National Pension Scheme for NRI
Any Indian citizen (resident or non-resident) or an Overseas Citizen of India (OCI) aged between 18-70 years is eligible for the NPS scheme. However, that person must also be compliant with the Know Your Customer (KYC) norms.
Persons of Indian Origin (PIOs) or Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) are not eligible for the NPS scheme for NRI. It’s an individual account that cannot be opened on behalf of another person. Moreover, the applicant must be legally competent to execute a contract as per the Indian Contract Act.
Here are some other considerations to keep in mind:
Submission of PAN and compliance with full KYC norms are mandatory. KYC can be completed with Aadhaar, a passport, or supporting address and identity proofs.
Only NRE or NRO accounts may be linked for NPS contributions. Fund transfers into NPS must comply with RBI and FEMA regulations.
How to Invest in NPS as an NRI – Step-by-Step Guide
NPS is usually distributed through authorised entities called Points of Presence (POP). Almost all the private and public sector banks in India can act as a Point of Presence under this retirement fund plan.
On that note, here is a step-by-step guide to investing in NPS as an NRI:
Visit the official eNPS website.
Click on the ‘National Pension System’ option and select ‘Registration.’
Navigate to the ‘Status of Applicant’ field and choose ‘Non-resident of India.’
Go to the ‘Register with’ option, select ‘Permanent Account Number’ and register using your PAN card.
Provide additional details, such as your NRE or NRO account, bank account number, passport number, and current country of residence.
Click on continue and specify your investment details.
Select the scheme and your pension fund manager.
Upload all the scanned copies of your required documents, photograph, and signature.
Complete the form and collect its hard copy.
Sign the printed form and send it to the CRA within 90 days of registering for NPS.
Investment Options & Fund Management for NRIs under NPS
NRIs investing in NPS can select from multiple fund options and asset allocations. The retirement plan offers solutions for varying risk appetites and retirement goals. The two primary investment strategies are Active Choice (individual funds) and Auto Choice (lifecycle fund), each with distinct control levels and equity exposure limits.
In Active Choice investments, the subscriber can actively decide how their contributions are to be invested, based on personal preferences. Here is the equity allocation matrix for this choice:
In the Auto Choice fund, investments are spread across three asset classes based on a predefined portfolio that typically adjusts according to the subscriber’s age. It has three types: The aggressive life cycle fund, which allows a cap of 75% of the total assets for equity investment, the moderate life cycle fund with a cap of 50%, and the conservative life cycle fund with a cap of 25% of total assets.
Withdrawal Rules for National Pension Scheme NRI Accounts
The NPS for NRIs offers flexible but regulated withdrawal options depending on age, exit timing, and circumstances. Withdrawals are governed by rules to ensure long-term retirement security.
At maturity (age 60), partial lump sum and mandatory annuity purchase are allowed. Premature exits involve stricter annuitization requirements. As mentioned earlier, nominees can claim full benefits in case of death. Subscribers can also defer withdrawals and continue investing up to age 75.
Meanwhile, here are the key NPS withdrawal rules for NRIs:
At Retirement (60 years): Up to 60% lump sum, minimum 40% annuity. If corpus ≤ ₹2 lakh, full withdrawal allowed.
Early Exit (Before 60 years): Up to 20% lump sum, minimum 80% annuity. If corpus ≤ ₹1 lakh, full withdrawal allowed.
Deferment Option: Continue till 75; defer lump sum to 70, annuity by 3 years.
On Death: Nominee gets 100% lump sum.
Here’s an overview of the withdrawal summary table:
Benefits of NPS for NRIs – Why Choose It?
The NPS scheme for NRIs offers several benefits for interested subscribers and investors, such as:
Market-Linked Returns & Inflation Hedge
NPS invests contributions across equities, corporate bonds, and government securities. The scheme offers market-linked returns instead of fixed, low-interest earnings. Over the long term, this helps NRI investors build a retirement corpus that can grow faster than inflation. It further leads to real wealth preservation and a higher standard of living post-retirement.
Low-Cost, Efficient Fund Management
The scheme regulated by the PFRDA boasts exceptionally low fund management charges (as low as 0.01%). This makes it one of the most cost-efficient pension products globally. It also ensures more of the investment goes towards wealth creation, not management expenses.
Long-Term Retirement Income via Annuity
At maturity (age 60 or above), an accumulated corpus of up to 60% can be withdrawn tax-free in India, while the remaining 40% must be used to purchase an annuity. This provides NRIs with a steady retirement income for life. It means they will continue to receive a regular pension post-retirement that supports lifelong financial security.
Flexibility in Contributions and Account Management
NPS offers flexibility in frequency and quantum of contributions, with minimum annual contributions (as low as ₹1,000) and no cap on the maximum. NRIs can contribute as per their financial situation, choose their asset allocation style, and even switch fund managers. This helps with easy account management across countries and time zones.
Tax Benefits
Contributions to NPS qualify for deductions under Section 80C (up to ₹1.5 lakh) and Section 80CCD(1B) (an additional ₹50,000) for NRIs with taxable Indian income. Over 60% of the final corpus withdrawn is also tax-exempt in India, and the annuity ensures a stable, predictable post-retirement income stream.
Portability and Global Access
The NPS account remains operational and fully accessible regardless of where the NRI resides globally. There’s no need to close or reopen the account if residential status changes, and contributions can be made securely through NRE or NRO bank accounts.
Tax Benefits of the National Pension Scheme for NRIs
NRIs investing in the NPS scheme enjoy significant tax benefits under various sections of the Income Tax Act. They can easily reduce their taxable income while building a retirement corpus in India.
Section 80CCD (1):
NRIs can claim deductions for personal contributions to NPS up to 10% of salary (if salaried) or 20% of gross total income (if self-employed), subject to an overall ceiling of ₹1.5 lakh under Section 80CCE.
Section 80CCD(1B):
Offers an additional exclusive deduction of up to ₹50,000 specifically for NPS contributions over and above the ₹1.5 lakh limit, increasing total potential deductions to ₹2 lakhs.
Key Takeaways
NRIs can invest in the NPS scheme in India and build up savings for retirement.
The NPS for NRI accounts is a combination of investment instruments and asset classes like equity, government securities, and corporate bonds.
Since you will be an individual subscriber, your choice of investment will always be according to your risk appetite.
You can exit from the NPS scheme before 60 years.
In case of an unfortunate event, like death, the nominee can receive 100% of the NPS pension wealth as a lump sum.
Conclusion
The National Pension Scheme for NRI is an excellent plan for retirement in India. The advantages of this plan are tax benefits, flexibility, market-related returns, and portability. The requirement for subscribing to this retirement plan is an NRE or an NRO account. You can also create a substantial retirement corpus and get the advantages associated with regular income through annuity plans with small contributions to this retirement fund plan. You may also use a retirement calculator to plan your contributions effectively.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) on the National Pension Scheme for NRI
Can NRIs open NPS accounts and continue contributing while abroad?
Yes, NRIs can open NPS accounts and continue making contributions while abroad. However, they must meet eligibility criteria, have an Indian PAN, including an NRE or NRO account for contribution, and maintain an Indian bank account.
Is a Tier II NPS account available for NRIs?
No. NRIs cannot open a Tier II NPS account because it is restricted to resident Indians under the current regulations. NRIs can only open a Tier I account for retirement planning in the country.
How are NPS withdrawals from NRI accounts taxed in India and abroad?
The lump sum withdrawal at retirement (up to 60%) is usually tax-exempt. For partial withdrawals, up to 25% of contributions are allowed to be withdrawn tax-free after three years.
What happens to my NPS account if I become an OCI?
Your NPS account remains active and functions similarly to that of an NRI even after you become an OCI. This means you can continue with your investments until retirement. The only condition is that you must have an NRE or NRO account to avail of the scheme.
Can NRIs switch fund managers and asset allocations? How often?
According to the latest circular of the PFRDA, NPS subscribers can easily select up to three pension fund managers for different asset classes, including equity, government bonds, corporate bonds, and corporate asset class. For new subscribers, this option will be available three months after NPS registration.
What documents are mandatory to maintain an NPS account as an NRI?
The following documents are mandatory for maintaining a National Pension Scheme for NRI account:
Filled in the subscriber registration form
Copy of Passport
Proof of Address, if the local address is different from the address in the passport
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility
full_pdf_text:
National Pension Scheme for NRI
The National Pension Scheme for NRI was introduced for government employees in 20041 to provide them with financial security after retirement. Later on, this retirement planning scheme was available for all Indian citizens, including employees in the private sector, self-employed individuals, and non-resident Indians (NRIs). The plan systematically urges people to start saving from an early age and plan their retirement accordingly. It often focuses on accumulating regular contributions to your retirement corpus through low-cost investments.
In this guide, we will discuss more about the features of NPS for NRIs, investment opportunities, and benefits in detail.
What is the National Pension Scheme for NRI?
The National Pension Scheme for NRI is a retirement plan introduced by the Government of India as a part of pension sector reforms. The scheme is a long-term, voluntary retirement savings plan in India which is administered and regulated by the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA). Any NRI who holds a non-resident external (NRE) or non-resident ordinary (NRO) account can invest in NPS to generate a retirement corpus, which is a combination of equity, government securities, and debt. This ensures minimal impact on returns for subscriber contributions, even if the market is down.
In short, an NPS for NRI scheme allows non-resident Indians to accumulate a safe retirement fund in India and incur tax advantages accordingly. Another advantage is that it offers multiple possibilities for NRIs because of its structured investment options.
When the person reaches 60 years, they can easily withdraw up to 60% of the maturity benefit in a single lump sum payment. They can also use the remaining 40% to purchase another annuity scheme for a regular pension.
Key Features of the NPS for NRI Account
The primary objective of the National Pension Scheme for NRI is to promote savings and investment and help individuals plan their retirement. Meanwhile, the following features make it an excellent choice for long-term wealth accumulation:
1. Flexibility and Portability
The Central Recordkeeping Agency (CRA) issues a Permanent Retirement Account Number (PRAN) to each subscriber of the NPS scheme. This helps them maintain their respective databases along with all recorded transactions until their retirement. PRAN ensures portability and continuity of the pension account across employment, geographies, and sector changes.
Another positive part about the NPS scheme for NRI is that the PRAN remains the same for the subscriber’s lifetime and ensures efficient portability if someone is moving to a new location in India.
2. Voluntary Contributions
The National Pension Scheme for NRI allows subscribers to participate in investments voluntarily according to their financial capacity. Unlike traditional pension schemes, this retirement plan allows investors to contribute as and when convenient. The minimum investment expected from the NRIs is minimum ₹500 for each transaction and ₹1000 annually2, while opening a Tier I account.
There is no cap on the invested amount, which makes NPS an excellent retirement planning scheme for individuals with higher net worth.
3. Investment Portfolio Options
NRIs can easily customise their portfolios, thanks to the flexibility offered by NPS. This allows better diversification and risk management. The scheme provides multiple investment portfolio options for the subscribers, such as Equity (E), Corporate Bonds (C) and /or Government Securities (G).
The equity option allows investments in stock-market-linked instruments for better long- term returns. Conversely, NRIs can also invest in high-rated corporate bonds issued by several reputed Indian corporates. The Government securities option, on the other hand, focuses on investments across central bonds and treasury bills, which are often considered less risky instruments.
4. Partial Withdrawals and Exits
NPS for NRI also allows partial withdrawal from the account if needed. Under the Tier I scheme, you will be allowed to withdraw a maximum of 25% of your contributions. The same rule applies if your employer also contributes to your account. There are no such restrictions if you have a Tier II account. However, the amount withdrawn within 3 years is taxable in the year of withdrawal for employees who have claimed deductions under Section 80C.
Eligibility Criteria for National Pension Scheme for NRI
Any Indian citizen (resident or non-resident) or an Overseas Citizen of India (OCI) aged between 18- 70 years3 is eligible for the NPS scheme. However, that person must also be compliant with the Know Your Customer (KYC) norms.
Persons of Indian Origin (PIOs) or Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) are not eligible for the NPS scheme for NRI. It’s an individual account that cannot be opened on behalf of another person. Moreover, the applicant must be legally competent to execute a contract as per the Indian Contract Act, 1872.
Here are some other considerations to keep in mind4:
1. Compliance with KYC norms are mandatory. This includes submission of a valid Indian
passport, address proof, and other important documents.
2. Only NRE or NRO accounts may be linked for NPS contributions. Fund transfers into NPS
must comply with RBI and FEMA regulations.
How to Invest in NPS as an NRI – Step-by-Step Guide
NPS is usually distributed through authorised entities called Points of Presence (POP). Almost all the private and public sector banks in India can act as a Point of Presence under this retirement fund plan.
On that note, here is a step-by-step guide to investing in NPS as an NRI:
1. Visit the official eNPS website. 2. Click on the ‘National Pension System’ option and select ‘Registration.’ 3. Navigate to the ‘Status of Applicant’ field and choose ‘Non-resident of India.’ 4. Go to the ‘Register with’ option, select ‘Permanent Account Number’ and register using your
PAN card. 5. Provide additional details, such as your NRE or NRO account, bank account number, passport
number, and current country of residence. 6. Click on continue and specify your investment details. 7. Select the scheme and your pension fund manager. 8. Upload all the scanned copies of your required documents, photograph, and signature. 9. Complete the form and collect its hard copy. 10. Sign the printed form and send it to the CRA within 90 days of registering for NPS.
Investment Options & Fund Management for NRIs under NPS
NRIs investing in NPS can select from multiple fund options and asset allocations. The retirement plan offers solutions for varying risk appetites and retirement goals. The two primary investment strategies are Active Choice (individual funds) and Auto Choice (lifecycle fund), each with distinct control levels and equity exposure limits.
In Active Choice investments, the subscriber can actively decide how their contributions are to be invested, based on personal preferences. Here is the equity allocation matrix for this choice:
Age (years) Max. Equity Allocation
Upto 50 75%
51 72.50%
52 70%
53 67.50%
54 65%
55 62.50%
56 60%
57 57.50%
58 55%
59 52.50%
60 & above 50%
In the Auto Choice fund, investments are spread across three asset classes based on a predefined portfolio that typically adjusts according to the subscriber’s age. It has three types: The aggressive life cycle fund, which allows a cap of 75% of the total assets for equity investment, the moderate life cycle fund with a cap of 50%, and the conservative life cycle fund with a cap of 25% of total assets.
Withdrawal Rules for National Pension Scheme NRI Accounts
The NPS for NRIs offers flexible but regulated withdrawal options depending on age, exit timing, and circumstances. Withdrawals are governed by rules to ensure long-term retirement security.
At maturity (age 60), partial lump sum and mandatory annuity purchase are allowed. Premature exits involve stricter annuitization requirements. As mentioned earlier, nominees can claim full benefits in case of death. Subscribers can also defer withdrawals and continue investing up to age 75.
Meanwhile, here are the key NPS withdrawal rules for NRIs5:
● At Retirement (60 years): Up to 60% lump sum, minimum 40% annuity. If corpus ≤ ₹2 lakh, full withdrawal allowed. ● Early Exit (Before 60 years): Up to 20% lump sum, minimum 80% annuity. If corpus ≤ ₹1 lakh, full withdrawal allowed. ● Deferment Option: Continue till 75; defer lump sum to 70, annuity by 3 years. ● On Death: Nominee gets 100% lump sum.
Here’s an overview of the withdrawal summary table5:
Scenario Lump Sum Annuity Special Rule
At 60+ Max 60% Min 40% Full withdrawal if ≤ ₹2Lakh
Early Exit Max 20% Min 80% Full withdrawal if ≤ ₹1Lakh
Deferment Till 70 Till +3 years Invest till 75
Death 100% Nil Paid to the nominee
Benefits of NPS for NRIs – Why Choose It?
The NPS scheme for NRIs offers several benefits for interested subscribers and investors, such as:
1. Market-Linked Returns & Inflation Hedge
NPS invests contributions across equities, corporate bonds, and government securities. The scheme offers market-linked returns instead of fixed, low-interest earnings. Over the long term, this helps NRI investors build a retirement corpus that can grow faster than inflation. It further leads to real wealth preservation and a higher standard of living post-retirement.
2. Low-Cost, Efficient Fund Management
The scheme regulated by the PFRDA boasts exceptionally low fund management charges (as low as 0.01%). This makes it one of the most cost-efficient pension products globally. It also ensures more of the investment goes towards wealth creation, not management expenses.
3. Long-Term Retirement Income via Annuity
At maturity (age 60 or above), an accumulated corpus of up to 60% can be withdrawn tax- free in India, while the remaining 40% must be used to purchase an annuity. This provides
NRIs with a steady retirement income for life. It means they will continue to receive a regular pension post-retirement that supports lifelong financial security.
4. Flexibility in Contributions and Account Management
NPS offers flexibility in frequency and quantum of contributions, with minimum annual contributions (as low as ₹1,000) and no cap on the maximum. NRIs can contribute as per their financial situation, choose their asset allocation style, and even switch fund managers. This helps with easy account management across countries and time zones.
5. Tax Benefits
Contributions to NPS qualify for deductions under Section 80C (up to ₹1.5 lakh) and Section 80CCD(1B) (an additional ₹50,000) for NRIs with taxable Indian income. Over 60% of the final corpus withdrawn is also tax-exempt in India, and the annuity ensures a stable, predictable post-retirement income stream.
6. Portability and Global Access
The NPS account remains operational and fully accessible regardless of where the NRI resides globally. There’s no need to close or reopen the account if residential status changes, and contributions can be made securely through NRE or NRO bank accounts.
Tax Benefits of the National Pension Scheme for NRIs
NRIs investing in the NPS scheme enjoy significant tax benefits under various sections of the Income Tax Act. They can easily reduce their taxable income while building a retirement corpus in India.
1. Section 80C
NRIs can claim deductions for personal contributions to NPS scheme up to a limit of ₹1.5 lakhs in a financial year.4
2. Section 80CCD(1B):
Offers an additional exclusive deduction of up to ₹50,000 specifically for NPS contributions over and above the ₹1.5 lakh limit, increasing total potential deductions to ₹2 lakhs4.
Key Takeaways
1. NRIs can invest in the NPS scheme in India and build up savings for retirement. 2. The NPS for NRI accounts is a combination of investment instruments and asset classes like
equity, government securities, and corporate bonds. 3. Since you will be an individual subscriber, your choice of investment will always be according
to your risk appetite. 4. You can exit from the NPS scheme before 60 years. 5. In case of an unfortunate event, like death, the nominee can receive 100% of the NPS
pension wealth as a lump sum.
Conclusion
The National Pension Scheme for NRI is an excellent plan for retirement in India. The advantages of this plan are tax benefits, flexibility, market-related returns, and portability. The requirement for subscribing to this retirement plan is an NRE or an NRO account. You can also create a substantial retirement corpus and get the advantages associated with regular income through annuity plans with small contributions to this retirement fund plan. You may also use a retirement calculator to plan your contributions effectively.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) on the National Pension Scheme for NRI
1. Can NRIs open NPS accounts and continue contributing while abroad?
Yes, NRIs can open NPS accounts and continue making contributions while abroad. However, they must meet eligibility criteria, have an Indian PAN, including an NRE or NRO account for contribution, and maintain an Indian bank account.
2. Is a Tier II NPS account available for NRIs?
No. NRIs cannot open a Tier II NPS account because it is restricted to resident Indians under the current regulations4. NRIs can only open a Tier I account for retirement planning in the country.
3. How are NPS withdrawals from NRI accounts taxed in India and abroad?
The lump sum withdrawal at retirement (up to 60%) is usually tax-exempt4. For partial withdrawals, up to 25% of contributions are allowed to be withdrawn tax-free after three years4.
4. What happens to my NPS account if I become an OCI (Overseas Citizen of India)?
Your NPS account remains active and functions similarly to that of an NRI even after you become an OCI.
5. What documents are mandatory to maintain an NPS account as an NRI?
The following documents are mandatory for maintaining a National Pension Scheme for NRI account6:
○ Filled in the subscriber registration form ○ Copy of Passport ○ Proof of Address, if the local address is different from the address in the passport

changes.word_original: The National Pension Scheme for NRI was introduced for government employees in 2004 to provide them with financial security after retirement.
changes.pdf_final: The National Pension Scheme for NRI was introduced for government employees in 20041 to provide them with financial security after retirement.

changes.word_original: Starting in 2009, this retirement planning scheme was available for all Indian citizens, including employees in the private sector, self-employed individuals, and non-resident Indians (NRIs).
changes.pdf_final: Later on, this retirement planning scheme was available for all Indian citizens, including employees in the private sector, self-employed individuals, and non-resident Indians (NRIs).

changes.word_original: The minimum investment expected from the NRIs is ₹500 for each transaction and ₹1000 annually, while opening a Tier I account.
changes.pdf_final: The minimum investment expected from the NRIs is minimum ₹500 for each transaction and ₹1000 annually2, while opening a Tier I account.

changes.word_original: The equity option allows investments in stock-market-linked instruments for better long-term returns.
changes.pdf_final: The equity option allows investments in stock-market-linked instruments for better long- term returns.

changes.word_original: Any Indian citizen (resident or non-resident) or an Overseas Citizen of India (OCI) aged between 18-70 years is eligible for the NPS scheme.
changes.pdf_final: Any Indian citizen (resident or non-resident) or an Overseas Citizen of India (OCI) aged between 18- 70 years3 is eligible for the NPS scheme.

changes.word_original: Moreover, the applicant must be legally competent to execute a contract as per the Indian Contract Act.
changes.pdf_final: Moreover, the applicant must be legally competent to execute a contract as per the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

changes.word_original: Here are some other considerations to keep in mind:
changes.pdf_final: Here are some other considerations to keep in mind4:

changes.word_original: Submission of PAN and compliance with full KYC norms are mandatory.
KYC can be completed with Aadhaar, a passport, or supporting address and identity proofs.
changes.pdf_final: Compliance with KYC norms are mandatory.
This includes submission of a valid Indian
passport, address proof, and other important documents.

changes.word_original: Fund transfers into NPS must comply with RBI and FEMA regulations.
changes.pdf_final: Fund transfers into NPS
must comply with RBI and FEMA regulations.

changes.word_original: Visit the official eNPS website.
Click on the ‘National Pension System’ option and select ‘Registration.’
changes.pdf_final: Visit the official eNPS website. 2.

changes.word_original: Navigate to the ‘Status of Applicant’ field and choose ‘Non-resident of India.’
changes.pdf_final: Click on the ‘National Pension System’ option and select ‘Registration.’ 3.

changes.word_original: Go to the ‘Register with’ option, select ‘Permanent Account Number’ and register using your PAN card.
changes.pdf_final: Navigate to the ‘Status of Applicant’ field and choose ‘Non-resident of India.’ 4.

changes.word_original: Provide additional details, such as your NRE or NRO account, bank account number, passport number, and current country of residence.
changes.pdf_final: Go to the ‘Register with’ option, select ‘Permanent Account Number’ and register using your

changes.word_original: Click on continue and specify your investment details.
changes.pdf_final: PAN card. 5.

changes.word_original: Select the scheme and your pension fund manager.
changes.pdf_final: Provide additional details, such as your NRE or NRO account, bank account number, passport

changes.word_original: Upload all the scanned copies of your required documents, photograph, and signature.
changes.pdf_final: number, and current country of residence. 6.

changes.word_original: Complete the form and collect its hard copy.
changes.pdf_final: Click on continue and specify your investment details. 7.
Select the scheme and your pension fund manager. 8.
Upload all the scanned copies of your required documents, photograph, and signature. 9.
Complete the form and collect its hard copy. 10.

changes.word_original: Meanwhile, here are the key NPS withdrawal rules for NRIs:
changes.pdf_final: Meanwhile, here are the key NPS withdrawal rules for NRIs5:

changes.word_original: At Retirement (60 years): Up to 60% lump sum, minimum 40% annuity.
changes.pdf_final: ● At Retirement (60 years): Up to 60% lump sum, minimum 40% annuity.

changes.word_original: If corpus ≤ ₹2 lakh, full withdrawal allowed.
changes.pdf_final: If corpus ≤ ₹2 lakh, full withdrawal allowed. ● Early Exit (Before 60 years): Up to 20% lump sum, minimum 80% annuity.

changes.word_original: Early Exit (Before 60 years): Up to 20% lump sum, minimum 80% annuity.
changes.pdf_final: If corpus ≤ ₹1 lakh, full withdrawal allowed. ● Deferment Option: Continue till 75; defer lump sum to 70, annuity by 3 years. ● On Death: Nominee gets 100% lump sum.

changes.word_original: If corpus ≤ ₹1 lakh, full withdrawal allowed.
changes.pdf_final: Here’s an overview of the withdrawal summary table5:

changes.word_original: Deferment Option: Continue till 75; defer lump sum to 70, annuity by 3 years.
changes.pdf_final: Scenario Lump Sum Annuity Special Rule

changes.word_original: On Death: Nominee gets 100% lump sum.
changes.pdf_final: At 60+ Max 60% Min 40% Full withdrawal if ≤ ₹2Lakh

changes.word_original: Here’s an overview of the withdrawal summary table:
changes.pdf_final: Early Exit Max 20% Min 80% Full withdrawal if ≤ ₹1Lakh
Deferment Till 70 Till +3 years Invest till 75
Death 100% Nil Paid to the nominee

changes.word_original: At maturity (age 60 or above), an accumulated corpus of up to 60% can be withdrawn tax-free in India, while the remaining 40% must be used to purchase an annuity.
changes.pdf_final: At maturity (age 60 or above), an accumulated corpus of up to 60% can be withdrawn tax- free in India, while the remaining 40% must be used to purchase an annuity.

changes.word_original: This provides NRIs with a steady retirement income for life.
changes.pdf_final: This provides
NRIs with a steady retirement income for life.

changes.word_original: Section 80CCD (1):

NRIs can claim deductions for personal contributions to NPS up to 10% of salary (if salaried) or 20% of gross total income (if self-employed), subject to an overall ceiling of ₹1.5 lakh under Section 80CCE.
changes.pdf_final: Section 80C
NRIs can claim deductions for personal contributions to NPS scheme up to a limit of ₹1.5 lakhs in a financial year.4

changes.word_original: Offers an additional exclusive deduction of up to ₹50,000 specifically for NPS contributions over and above the ₹1.5 lakh limit, increasing total potential deductions to ₹2 lakhs.
changes.pdf_final: Offers an additional exclusive deduction of up to ₹50,000 specifically for NPS contributions over and above the ₹1.5 lakh limit, increasing total potential deductions to ₹2 lakhs4.

changes.word_original: NRIs can invest in the NPS scheme in India and build up savings for retirement.
The NPS for NRI accounts is a combination of investment instruments and asset classes like equity, government securities, and corporate bonds.
changes.pdf_final: NRIs can invest in the NPS scheme in India and build up savings for retirement. 2.

changes.word_original: Since you will be an individual subscriber, your choice of investment will always be according to your risk appetite.
changes.pdf_final: The NPS for NRI accounts is a combination of investment instruments and asset classes like

changes.word_original: You can exit from the NPS scheme before 60 years.
changes.pdf_final: equity, government securities, and corporate bonds. 3.

changes.word_original: In case of an unfortunate event, like death, the nominee can receive 100% of the NPS pension wealth as a lump sum.
changes.pdf_final: Since you will be an individual subscriber, your choice of investment will always be according
to your risk appetite. 4.
You can exit from the NPS scheme before 60 years. 5.
In case of an unfortunate event, like death, the nominee can receive 100% of the NPS
pension wealth as a lump sum.

changes.word_original: NRIs cannot open a Tier II NPS account because it is restricted to resident Indians under the current regulations.
changes.pdf_final: NRIs cannot open a Tier II NPS account because it is restricted to resident Indians under the current regulations4.

changes.word_original: The lump sum withdrawal at retirement (up to 60%) is usually tax-exempt.
changes.pdf_final: The lump sum withdrawal at retirement (up to 60%) is usually tax-exempt4.

changes.word_original: For partial withdrawals, up to 25% of contributions are allowed to be withdrawn tax-free after three years.
changes.pdf_final: For partial withdrawals, up to 25% of contributions are allowed to be withdrawn tax-free after three years4.

changes.word_original: What happens to my NPS account if I become an OCI?

changes.pdf_final: What happens to my NPS account if I become an OCI (Overseas Citizen of India)?

changes.word_original: This means you can continue with your investments until retirement.
The only condition is that you must have an NRE or NRO account to avail of the scheme.
